title: Frontier Trail Encounters
description: The wide open country between towns — cattle trails, mountain passes, river crossings, and the long miles of dust and distance that define the frontier. What you find on the road is rarely just road.

entries.result: A rider approaching from the opposite direction who pulls off the trail a hundred yards ahead and waits for the party to pass without acknowledging them.
A stagecoach stopped at the side of the trail, horses standing easy, no driver on the box. The curtains are drawn.
Fresh wagon tracks leading off the trail into brush in a direction that makes no geographic sense.
A cattle herd moving across the trail — not along it, across it — with two riders pushing them and no explanation offered.
A freshly turned mound of earth beside the trail with a wooden marker that says only a name and 'Died Honest.'
A pack mule grazing loose, loaded, no owner anywhere visible.
Three men eating a quiet lunch by the trail who track the party's approach without appearing to, and who are very still until the party is past.
A fire pit still warm, three bedrolls, gear for a camp of three. No people. A half-cooked meal on the coals.
A dead horse — recently, fully rigged — on its side in the trail. The saddlebags are still on. They are not empty.
Smoke rising from beyond a ridge line at a volume that suggests more than a cook fire.
A man walking — no horse, no visible water, no hat — who accepts help with a specific request and declines to explain anything else.
A section of trail where every rock in a quarter-mile stretch has been turned over. Methodically. Recently.
A burned wagon carcass. The ash is cold. The wheel iron is intact. The cargo is gone.
A creek crossing with fresher tracks going in on the other side than the tracks going in on this side. Someone doubled back, walked the water.
A rider at full gallop disappearing over a rise, not looking back, pushing a horse harder than distance requires.
A trail marker that's been turned around to point the wrong direction. Deliberately and recently.
A camp with good ground, water nearby, wood close — the kind of spot experienced travelers use. Three sets of tracks arrive. Two sets leave.
A stretch of trail where the grass on both sides has been heavily disturbed by movement — a large group, moving fast, off the trail.
A woman riding alone, traveling the same direction, at a pace that suggests she is not in a hurry but is watching the trail behind her.
A buckboard wagon with a broken axle, driver doing repairs alone, carrying cargo he's not describing and is keeping covered.
A U.S. Marshal camped at a watering hole, friendly, reading the same wanted notice three times without seeming to.
A message scratched into a rock face beside the trail, in a language that most people in this territory shouldn't know.
A toll being collected on a bridge by two men with rifles and a handwritten sign. The toll amount is not printed anywhere visible.
An old sheepherder who knows every trail in this range, asks only where the party came from and where they're headed, and listens carefully to both answers.
A ford that's been recently tampered with — stones repositioned, the depth made deceptive — in a manner that would trap a loaded wagon.
A buzzard circle at a site the trail doesn't pass near. Low. Patient. Not bluffing.
A group of five riders camped off the trail who explain they're waiting for someone who was supposed to meet them here yesterday.
An abandoned line shack — roof sound, supplies inside, someone's been using it recently — with a name carved repeatedly into the doorframe.
A rider coming from the opposite direction who warns the party about trouble ahead and rides past before anyone can ask what kind.
An arrow marker on the trail — not a sign, an actual arrow, stuck in the dirt, pointing off the established path.
A shallow grave dug up and left open, empty, beside the trail. The digging is old. The disturbance is new.
A horse still saddled, still tied to a mesquite, standing at a crossroads. Hours at least.
Two riders traveling the opposite direction who ask whether the party has seen a third rider — describe them accurately — and move on when the answer is no.
A stretch of trail where the ground has been swept with a branch, obscuring tracks for about fifty yards.
A prospector heading out, not in, loaded heavy, asking for the current price of silver in the nearest town without giving a reason for asking.
A dead campfire near a good water source where someone slept recently and left a hat behind. Good hat.
A string of horses — five of them, tied in a line — hitched to a tree at the edge of a canyon, no rider, no camp visible.
A printed broadside nailed to a trail marker about a man wanted in a neighboring territory, with a reward large enough to be meaningful and a description accurate enough to be useful.
A military patrol — small, professional — going the same direction, asking where the party is headed and who authorized the travel.
A wagon loaded with furniture and household goods, driven by a family going somewhere with quiet urgency and no conversation.
A telegraph wire cut at a pole beside the trail. Recently. The cut end is on the ground.
A group of travelers from back east — new clothes, soft hands — who are going somewhere inadvisable and don't know enough to know it.
A man sitting against a boulder at the trailside who is asleep, armed, and positioned to watch the trail in both directions if awake.
A wagon wheel detached and leaning against a tree with a note tucked into a spoke: a message in a handwriting no one in the party recognizes.
A spring that should have water, mapped as having water, that is dry. Recently and deliberately filled with rock.
A rider from a large outfit passing a message to the party: the outfit's boss wants a word with anyone heading to the next town. It's phrased as a request.
A section of trail in a narrow canyon where the rock on both sides bears recent tool marks — deliberate shaping — for no obvious purpose.
A group of missionaries heading into the territory with a wagon of supplies, kind intentions, and no functional awareness of the current situation.
An ambush site — studied correctly — abandoned before it was used, with spent tobacco and boot prints left behind.
A cattle brand burned into the bark of a notable tree at a crossroads. The brand belongs to a man who died two years ago.
A creek crossing where someone has built a serviceable ford out of local stone, without authorization, which everyone uses.
A woman riding toward town at a pace that says emergency with a rifle across the saddle and no saddlebags.
A man on foot leading a horse who won't talk about how he got into this situation but needs to get somewhere by dark.
A section of trail posted with a notice: private land, keep moving, no camping, no prospecting. The notice is handwritten. The posted land extends to the horizon.
A child sitting alone at the trailside holding a note with a name on it — a town, a person — waiting for someone going that direction.
A supply cache — food, ammunition, water — hidden under brush near the trail, recently placed, marked with a cut into a nearby rock.
A group of riders that has been paralleling the trail for the last hour on higher ground, matching pace, not approaching.
A stagecoach robbery site — obvious, recent — where the coach made it through and the would-be robbers didn't. One of them is still there.
A wanted man working a legitimate job — camp cook for a cattle drive — who makes eye contact with the party and says nothing.
A stretch of trail where the grass has not been disturbed — no tracks of any kind — when the trail should be well-traveled.
A man who offers to trade the party a secret for a favor. The secret is real. The favor is reasonable. He explains neither until the trade is accepted.
A cattle drive moving the opposite direction — three thousand head, eight riders — that will take an hour to pass on a trail wide enough for one.
A narrow pass through a canyon with fresh marks on both walls, as if something large was pushed through it not long ago.
An old Army trail marker indicating a fort that was decommissioned fifteen years ago, pointing accurately to a place that's now something else.
A traveling merchant with a mule string of goods, good prices, a memory for faces, and an interest in where people are coming from.
A trail that is on the map but is no longer a trail, grown over, which means either the map is old or someone made it grow over.
A lone rider circling a point a half-mile off the trail, methodically, for no obvious reason, for as long as the party watches.
A campsite with four bedrolls arranged around a fire, fully equipped, abandoned mid-meal. No sign of struggle. No sign of anything.
A man with a badge — old badge, old jurisdiction — doing something that's probably still legal and is definitely useful.
A section of trail recently graded — deliberately smoothed — in a way that would make it easier for a heavy wagon to travel fast and quiet.
A woman on a hilltop watching the trail with a spyglass. She watches until the party is past and then continues watching the direction they came from.
A fresh-dug hole — roughly chest-deep — near a distinctive rock formation, then filled back in. Recently.
A group of men who all work for the same outfit, traveling without cattle, all armed more than cattle work requires, who give a friendly and specific answer about their destination.
A hand-drawn map scratched into a flat rock at a fork in the trail, indicating a location two days ride from here.
A section of the trail posted as private by a land company, with a rider enforcing it, who is technically within his legal rights and knows it.
A peddler selling items that should not be available this far from a major settlement at prices that suggest he is moving them quickly.
A burned ranch house visible from the trail, recently, with nothing left standing. The corrals are intact. The livestock are gone.
A man who asks if the party will witness his signature on a document, hands them a copy, and rides away without another word.
A very old man at the trailside who has been in this territory since before any of the current players and offers information freely in exchange for news of the outside.
A spring wagon with a broken wheel and a load of mining equipment, the driver waiting for the right people to come along and help with a lift — and perhaps a proposition.
Tracks of a shod horse cutting off the trail and up a draw in a manner suggesting the rider knew exactly where they were going.
A rider who pulls up, studies the party for a moment, then hands over a letter addressed to someone by name and says it needs to reach them.
An area of ground that has been burned in a deliberate pattern — not wildfire, not campfire — covering about an acre.
Three men waiting at a water source who are polite, patient, and clearly waiting for someone specific before they'll let anyone water their animals.
A trail split not on any map, both paths equally traveled, with a marker that's been turned so many times it no longer points reliably.
A rider who takes the trail at a walk, one hand always visible, the other always near the saddle gun, watching everything.
An overturned supply wagon with its contents scattered. The driver is nowhere present. One wheel is still spinning.
A camp at a good water source that's been there a while, currently occupied by one person who has been watching the trail for days.
A stretch of the trail that runs through a dry creek bed, where the wagon ruts go in but don't come out the other side.
A very large man traveling alone, unarmed — visibly — who smiles easily and says the country ahead is fine, just fine, don't worry.
A downed tree across the trail, freshly cut. The cut end is fresh enough to still be bleeding sap.
A bank messenger — clearly — traveling alone on a route that should require an escort. He knows it should. He's nervous.
A woman with children, on foot, going toward town. She's not going to explain what she's coming from.
A set of tracks that goes off the trail at a dead run — multiple horses, close together — and doesn't come back.
A perfectly good rifle leaned against a trail marker. No one in any direction. Loaded. Cocked.
A man with significant injuries who says he's fine, needs nothing, and is walking to the next town. It's forty miles.
A stagecoach station that's been shut down — doors locked, corral empty — in the middle of a route that's supposed to be active.
A pair of riders who've been following the party at distance since the last water stop, neither gaining nor falling back.
An ambush — clear sign — set up for someone, in a good spot, currently unoccupied. Either the party arrived before the ambushers, or after.
The trail ahead is quiet in the way that experienced travelers know. Something has moved everything else off it, and that something has not moved off it yet.
